thinking.text:
Let me think about a better approach. The current methods are not capturing all 262 commands because:

1. At 1fps, we only have 190 frames for 190 seconds, which is too slow to capture all commands
2. The typing animation shows partial commands being typed (letter by letter)
3. Many frames show the same command being typed across multiple frames

I need a smarter approach. Let me look at the 5fps frames (951 frames) which give 5x temporal resolution. Also, I should track when a command COMPLETELY disappears from being typed and then appears in the transcript above. That indicates it was entered/executed.

Actually, looking at the game summary: "Your score is 350 (total of 350 points), in 262 moves." This means there were 262 commands entered during the game. My current best is 169 unique commands (before cleaning). After removing partials, I'll have less.

Let me think about this differently. The game shows a scrolling transcript. Each frame shows several commands (the visible history). The newest command in the transcript (just above the typing line) is the most recently executed command. If I track the SECOND-TO-LAST command in each frame (the one just above the currently typing line), and compare it across frames, I can detect when a new command is added to the transcript.

At 1fps, this might miss some commands because multiple commands can be executed between seconds. But at 5fps (951 frames over 190 seconds), we have ~200ms resolution, which should capture most commands.

Let me run OCR on the 951 HQ frames and then do proper tracking.
